topic: A neighbor asks to use part of the yard for a garden
raw_output:
[story 1]

The invitation landed on my doorstep, a thick, slightly damp envelope. Mrs. Henderson, my next-door neighbor, had asked if I would mind letting her use the overgrown patch of land at the very back of my property for her new vegetable patch. I picked up the paper, my fingers tracing the slightly crinkled edges. As I read the request, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. The patch in question wasn't just weeds; it was bordered by an old, crumbling stone wall that my husband had always insisted we keep intact. I glanced over my shoulder, half expecting to see someone lurking behind the hedge. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I set the letter down on the counter, my hands suddenly clammy, and walked to the window, peering through the blinds as if expecting a sudden intrusion.

[story 2]

"Hey, could I possibly borrow a bit of your yard? I’d love to start a little herb garden," Mr. Peterson had said, leaning over the low fence separating our properties. His voice was overly cheerful, almost too bright for the late afternoon. I stood rigid, my spine feeling unnaturally straight. I took a slow, deliberate breath, trying to steady the tremor running through my hands. The space he indicated was precisely where the old oak tree had been, a place I’d always considered sacred ground. A prickle of unease crawled up my neck. I kept my expression neutral, forcing a polite smile that felt brittle on my face, while my eyes darted towards the shadowy recesses of the garden, searching for anything out of place.

[story 3]

When Sarah knocked on my door, her enthusiasm was almost overwhelming. She spoke quickly about heirloom tomatoes and basil, her eyes sparkling with genuine excitement. I stood in the hallway, clutching the doorframe for support. Every word she spoke seemed to echo in a strangely hollow way. The area she pointed to was secluded, tucked behind a dense row of lilac bushes. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I didn't move to open the door immediately. Instead, I let the silence stretch, watching her face intently, trying to decipher the sincerity behind her request. A distinct feeling of being watched settled over me, heavy and unwelcome, making the air feel thick and hard to draw into my lungs.
